top_categories.workout_context: 94ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
At L'Etape Portugal, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of L'Etape Portugal. Train it until it feels boring.
At mile 60 of 87, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Race-pace efforts that mimic L'Etape Portugal's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before September.
8,202ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
L'Etape Portugal's paved — Portuguese municipal and national roads forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 87 miles.
8,202ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 87 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 8,202ft of climbing on paved — Portuguese municipal and national roads does to your power file on race day.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 87 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Tempo is L'Etape Portugal's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 87 miles on paved — Portuguese municipal and national roads. Train it until it's automatic.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for L'Etape Portugal’s 87 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Over Under, and G Spot to prepare you for 87 miles of paved — portuguese municipal and national roads in Viana do Castelo, Alto Minho, Northern Portugal.
